Companion Guide

How I use LLMs

Auto-generated with Gemini + Claude · ▶ Watch on YouTube

Introduction and Practical Applications

0:00
▶

This video transitions from the theoretical foundations of Large Language Models (LLMs) to their practical applications. While previous material covered the underlying training mechanisms and the conceptual "psychology" of these models, this section focuses on utility. The following content will demonstrate concrete examples, detail the available configuration settings, and illustrate practical workflows for integrating tools like ChatGPT into daily tasks.

The LLM Ecosystem Beyond ChatGPT

0:35

ChatGPT, developed by OpenAI and deployed in 2022, was the first application to successfully provide a text interface for interacting with a large language model. While it remains the incumbent—and is generally the most popular and feature-rich due to its maturity—the ecosystem has expanded significantly since its launch.

The landscape of notable LLM interfaces now includes offerings from both major technology companies and specialized startups:

  • Big Tech: Gemini (Google), Meta AI (Meta), and Copilot (Microsoft).
  • Startups: Claude (Anthropic), Grok (xAI), Perplexity, DeepSeek, and Le Chat (Mistral).

While many of these serve as direct alternatives to ChatGPT, several offer unique features and user experiences not found in the original platform. The relative performance and capabilities of these models are actively tracked via community leaderboards such as lmarena.ai/?leaderboard and scale.com/leaderboard.

Tracking LLM Performance with Leaderboards

2:04

The landscape of conversational AI includes numerous alternatives to ChatGPT from both large tech companies and startups. Key players include Google (Gemini), Meta, Microsoft (Copilot), Anthropic (Claude), xAI (Grok), DeepSeek, and Mistral (Le Chat).

To monitor the rapidly evolving performance of these models, community and enterprise leaderboards provide structured rankings:

  • Chatbot Arena: Provides rankings based on crowdsourced Elo scores derived from blind side-by-side comparisons of model outputs.
  • Scale SEAL Leaderboard: Offers rankings based on a suite of structured evaluations across various tasks.

While the ecosystem of models is diverse, OpenAI's platform often serves as the initial reference point due to its maturity and extensive feature set.

The Basic Interaction: Tokens and Text Generation

2:40
▶

The fundamental interaction with a language model consists of providing text as an input query and receiving generated text in response. Because these models are optimized for text generation, they perform well on tasks ranging from composing emails to writing poetry.

While user interfaces often present these interactions as conversational chat bubbles, the model does not process raw text strings directly. Instead, the input text is chopped into discrete, smaller segments called tokens. Both the user query and the model's response are represented internally as one-dimensional sequences of these tokens.

The exact tokenization process can be inspected using web tools like tiktokenizer. By selecting a specific model architecture, such as GPT-4, you can visualize how any given string is divided. The model operates on token IDs—integers that map to these text chunks—drawn from a predefined vocabulary. For GPT-4, this vocabulary contains approximately 200,000 possible tokens. This parsing process is highly specific and case-sensitive; altering a single character's capitalization or adding a space will change the resulting sequence of underlying token IDs.

Understanding the Conversation Format

4:44

While an isolated user query might be 15 tokens and the model's response 19 tokens, maintaining a conversation requires additional metadata. A specific chat format is used under the hood, injecting special tokens to delineate the roles and boundaries of each message.

What appears as separate chat bubbles in a user interface is actually a collaborative, one-dimensional token stream. The user contributes the initial tokens, and the model continues the sequence. Including the structural metadata, the entire haiku interaction forms a sequence of exactly 42 tokens:

<|im_start|>user<|im_sep|>Write a haiku about what it's like to be a Large Language Model.<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>assistant<|im_sep|>Words flow like a stream,
endless echoes, never mine—
ghost of thought, unseen.<|im_end|>

Special tokens like <|im_start|>, <|im_sep|>, and <|im_end|> explicitly define who is speaking and when their message concludes. Both the user and the assistant alternate writing into this shared token window.

A Mental Model for LLM Interaction

6:39

Interacting with an LLM involves collaboratively building a one-dimensional sequence of tokens. Initiating a "New chat" clears this sequence, resetting the conversation's state to zero.

The user begins by writing tokens into this stream. Pressing enter transfers control to the language model, which then responds by appending its own sequence of tokens. The model continues generation until it emits a special termination token indicating its turn is complete, at which point control transfers back to the user application.

This shared token stream is known as the context window. It functions as the working memory for the conversation; any information present within the context window is directly accessible by the model when generating subsequent responses.

How LLMs are Trained: Pre-training and Post-training

8:01

The training of a Large Language Model (LLM) is divided into two major stages: pre-training and post-training. The pre-training stage involves taking massive amounts of internet data, chopping it into tokens, and compressing it. This process creates a "lossy, probabilistic zip file" of the internet.

This compression is necessary because the sheer volume of internet information cannot be exactly represented within a single file, such as a one-terabyte zip file. Instead, the model captures the overall essence or patterns of the data. The contents of this "zip file" are the parameters of a neural network. A one-terabyte file, for example, corresponds roughly to one trillion parameters.

The neural network operates by taking a sequence of tokens and predicting the next token, using internet documents as its training data. By functioning as an internet document generator, the neural network acquires vast knowledge about the world, which is then compressed and stored within its parameters.

The Knowledge Cutoff and Post-Training Persona

9:28

The pre-training phase is a costly and lengthy process, often taking months and costing tens of millions of dollars. Because of this high expense, pre-training happens infrequently. Consequently, models have a knowledge cutoff corresponding to the end of their pre-training period, making their internal world knowledge slightly out of date. To handle recent information or current events, the model must rely on other mechanisms, such as external tool use, to provide that context dynamically.

Following pre-training is the post-training phase. Conceptually represented in the diagram as attaching a smiley face to the parameter "zip file," this stage shifts the model's behavior from simply generating raw internet documents to taking on the persona of a helpful assistant. This transformation is achieved by swapping the massive internet document dataset for a curated dataset of human-authored conversations. Post-training is a significantly cheaper fine-tuning process that relies on techniques like Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). While the model learns its conversational style during post-training, it draws upon the vast world knowledge acquired during pre-training to answer questions. The resulting artifact is, by default, a fully self-contained entity.

A Concise Introduction to an LLM

11:20

A Large Language Model (LLM) can be conceptualized as a fully self-contained, 1-terabyte file on a disk. This file contains the precise settings for roughly a trillion parameters within a neural network designed to predict the next token in a sequence. At its core, the model possesses no external tools—no calculator, Python interpreter, or web browsing capabilities. It functions solely by receiving a stream of input tokens and responding with a stream of output tokens.

The characteristics of this entity are shaped by two distinct training phases:

  • Pre-training: This phase compresses a vast amount of internet text into the model's parameters, forming its foundational knowledge. Because this process is lossy and the training data has a cutoff date, the model's knowledge is probabilistic, sometimes vague, and out of date. Concepts mentioned frequently on the internet are recalled with greater accuracy than rare topics.
  • Post-training: This phase, guided by human labelers, programs the model's "personality," style, and conversational format.

Practical Example: Knowledge-Based Queries

13:12

Consider a practical example of a knowledge-based query: asking an LLM, "How much caffeine is in one shot of Americano?"

This type of query is well-suited for a standard LLM prompt without external tool use for two primary reasons:

  1. Static Knowledge: The caffeine content of espresso is not recent or rapidly changing information.
  2. High Frequency in Training Data: This specific fact appears frequently across the internet. Due to the high number of occurrences in the training corpus, the model is likely to have a strong "memory" of this information encoded within its parameters.

When the model processes this prompt, it relies entirely on its internal weights to generate a response, stating roughly 63 milligrams.

However, it is crucial to remember that this output is not a guaranteed fact, but rather a probabilistic recollection of the training data. For definitive accuracy, one should always verify such claims against primary sources.

A Deeper Example: Medical Information and Verification

14:27

A practical application of querying the model's internal knowledge base involves seeking information on common topics, such as over-the-counter medications. When asked to evaluate DayQuil versus NyQuil for treating a runny nose, the model successfully broke down the active ingredients of each and assessed their efficacy for that specific symptom.

Because the model functions as a probabilistic recollection of its training data rather than a verified factual database, independent verification of its outputs is essential. In this scenario, verifying the model's claims involves cross-referencing the generated list of ingredients with the actual physical packaging of the medication. While the model is typically reliable when recalling ubiquitous, common knowledge—due to the sheer volume of such information in its training corpus—performing basic fact-checking remains a necessary safeguard against potential hallucinations, even in low-stakes situations.

Best Practice: Managing the Context Window

16:22

As you interact with LLMs, the sequence of tokens in your conversation grows longer. It is a best practice to always start a new chat when switching topics. Starting a new chat wipes the context window clean, resetting the token count to zero. Think of the tokens in the context window as the model's working memory—a precious resource that should not be overloaded with irrelevant information.

Keeping irrelevant tokens in the context window has two main drawbacks:

  • Distraction: A large number of past tokens can distract the model when it tries to sample new tokens, potentially decreasing accuracy and overall performance.
  • Computational cost: The more tokens present in the window, the more expensive and slightly slower it becomes to calculate and sample the next token in the sequence.

By keeping the context window as short and relevant as possible, you can expect the model to operate faster and generate higher-quality responses. However, if previous conversation turns are directly related to your current task, they should be retained in the context.

The Importance of Knowing Your Model and Pricing Tiers

18:03

It is crucial to be mindful of the specific model version interacting with your prompts. The active model is typically displayed in a drop-down menu at the top left of the interface. When using the service without logging in, the system defaults to a model, but does not explicitly state which one is active, often routing to a smaller, less capable version.

OpenAI currently structures access across three primary pricing tiers:

  • Free: Primarily relies on GPT-4o mini, a smaller model with fewer parameters. Compared to flagship models, it may exhibit lower creativity, less comprehensive knowledge, and a higher propensity for hallucinations. It offers limited access to larger models like GPT-4o and o3-mini.
  • Plus ($20/month): Grants access to the flagship GPT-4o model, subject to usage limits. For instance, current limits cap usage at 80 messages every three hours for GPT-4o.
  • Pro ($200/month): Provides unlimited access to GPT-4o alongside a suite of advanced features and early access previews.

The core takeaway is to align model selection with your specific needs. Larger models are more computationally expensive to run, which is reflected in the pricing tiers. Evaluate whether cheaper or free offerings suffice for casual use. However, for professional applications requiring high intelligence and reliability—such as software development—investing in top-tier models is often a necessary and worthwhile tradeoff.

Exploring Other LLM Providers and the "LLM Council"

20:53

Different LLM providers, such as Anthropic (Claude), Google (Gemini), and xAI (Grok), offer various pricing tiers that determine which models you can access. For instance, a paid subscription might grant access to Claude 3.5 Sonnet or Grok 3, whereas a free tier might restrict you to less capable models. It is generally recommended to select the most advanced model available within your budget that suits your specific use case.

A practical strategy for ideation and open-ended research is to consult an LLM council. This involves submitting the same prompt to multiple top-tier models across different providers and comparing their responses. For example, when seeking travel recommendations for a scenic, car-free city, querying both Claude and Gemini resulted in independent recommendations for Zermatt, Switzerland. Cross-referencing answers in this manner can help validate ideas and provide a more comprehensive perspective.

The Emergence of "Thinking Models" through Reinforcement Learning

22:54

The third major stage of training, following pre-training and supervised fine-tuning, is reinforcement learning (RL). If pre-training is akin to reading a textbook's exposition and supervised fine-tuning is studying worked examples, reinforcement learning corresponds to tackling practice problems.

During this phase, the model is provided with a problem statement and the known final answer. It is tasked with generating the intermediate solution steps. The process typically involves:

  • Generating multiple distinct solutions for a given prompt.
  • Filtering these solutions to identify those that arrive at the correct final answer.
  • Selecting the highest quality successful solutions (e.g., those that are correct and concise).
  • Training the model on these successful trajectories, repeating this process extensively.

A crucial outcome of this reinforcement learning phase is the emergence of what can be described as thinking strategies. Because the exact step-by-step thinking process is difficult for human labelers to hardcode, RL provides the environment for the model to discover effective strategies autonomously. The model learns to:

  • Try out different ideas.
  • Backtrack when an approach fails.
  • Revisit initial assumptions.

These learned behaviors closely resemble a human inner monologue during problem-solving. Recent research, notably highlighted by the paper DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning, demonstrates how models tuned with this approach develop an observable thinking process. When utilizing a thinking model, you can expect significantly higher accuracy, particularly on tasks demanding rigorous logic, such as mathematics and coding.

Introduction to 'Thinking' Models

25:00

The application of "thinking" phases during inference is highly beneficial for deep, complex problems, though it may be unnecessary for simple tasks. The trade-off for this capability is increased latency. Models will emit a large volume of tokens over several minutes as they "think" through a problem, simulating a human-like reasoning process. For difficult problems, this extended computation period can yield significantly higher accuracy.

Comparing Standard vs. Thinking Models on a Coding Problem

25:31

Standard models can struggle to identify the root cause of complex logic bugs, such as a failing gradient check in a multi-layer perceptron. When provided with the broken code, a flagship standard model like GPT-4o often returns a generic list of debugging suggestions rather than pinpointing the exact error.

By switching the model to a thinking variant—such as OpenAI's o1, o3-mini, or o1 Pro mode—the exact same prompt yields significantly better results. These models, tuned with reinforcement learning for advanced reasoning, spend time generating a sequence of thoughts before responding. When the failing code was submitted to o1 Pro mode, it analyzed the operations for about a minute and successfully identified the core issue: a parameter mismatch during variable packing and unpacking.

The prompt and the beginning of the submitted code are shown below:

My gradient check fails and I'm not sure why.

***
Exercise 3: Multilayer perceptron
So far, the parameters were only the weights in the weighted sum over some
set of basis functions, which was held fixed. The core idea of neural networks
is that we can learn the basis functions themselves, as well.
***

import numpy as np
from math import exp

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Generate some data: grently sloping, wavy, mildly quadratic, noisy

def make_data():
    rng = np.random.RandomState(42)
    x = np.linspace(0, 100, 15)
    trend = 0.1 * x # Linear trend
    oscillation = 2 * np.sin(x / 10) # Smooth wave
    noise = rng.uniform(-1.5, 1.5, size=len(x)) # Random noise
    quadratic = 0.001 * (x - 50)**2 # Subtle curvature
    y = trend + oscillation + noise + quadratic
    # Normalize x and y to be in range [-1, 1]
    minx, maxx = min(x), max(x)
    miny, maxy = min(y), max(y)
    x = 2 * (x - minx) / (maxx - minx) - 1

Testing Other LLMs (Claude, Gemini, Grok) on the Same Problem

27:46

The identical prompt was tested against several other LLMs. Claude 3.5 Sonnet successfully identified the correct issue and solved it, notably without being a specialized "thinking" model. It accurately diagnosed the problem with how the gradients were being packed and provided the corrected implementation.

def loss_and_grad(params):
    # unpack the parameters
    num_params = len(params)
    assert num_params % 3 == 0
    n_hidden = num_params // 3
    weights = params[::3]
    biases = params[1::3]
    output_weights = params[2::3]
    sigmoid = lambda x: 1 / (1 + exp(-x))
    loss = 0.0
    grads = [0.0 for _ in params]
    for i in range(N):
        xi, yi = x[i], y[i]
        # forward pass the network for this datapoint
        hidden = [sigmoid(w * xi + b) for w, b in zip(weights, biases)]
        output = sum(w * h for w, h in zip(output_weights, hidden))
        # calculate the loss
        loss += 1/N * (output - yi)**2
        # calculate the gradients
        doutput = 2/N * (output - yi)
        doutput_weights = [doutput * h for h in hidden]
        dhidden = [doutput * w for w in output_weights]
        dhidden = [dhidden[i] * (1 - hidden[i]) * hidden[i] for i in range(len(hidden))]
        dweights = [dhidden[i] * xi for i in range(len(hidden))]
        dbiases = [dhidden[i] for i in range(len(hidden))]

        # FIXED: correctly pack gradients to match parameter structure
        for j in range(n_hidden):
            grads[j*3] += dweights[j]        # weight gradients
            grads[j*3 + 1] += dbiases[j]     # bias gradients
            grads[j*3 + 2] += doutput_weights[j] # output weight gradients

    return loss, grads

The prompt was also tested on Gemini and Grok 3. Both models successfully solved the issue, demonstrating that this particular debugging task does not strictly require an extended reasoning model.

Using Perplexity.ai with the DeepSeek Reasoning Model

28:37

Perplexity.ai hosts the DeepSeek R1 model, making it readily accessible from their model dropdown. While the interface may require expanding a section to view the "raw thoughts," inspecting this reasoning trace provides insight into how the model works through a problem step-by-step. In the debugging scenario shown, the model explicitly steps through the forward and backward passes before identifying a critical mistake in the gradient accumulation logic.

Here is the plotting code visible in the prompt context above the reasoning trace:

ys.append(output)
plt.plot(xs, ys, color='red', label="Fitted Model")
plt.title("Data and Fitted Model")
plt.xlabel("x")
plt.ylabel("y")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

This highlights the utility of a class of models known as "thinking models." These models are optimized for difficult problems involving math, code, and logical deduction. They trade immediate response times for higher accuracy by spending time reasoning before outputting an answer. A practical heuristic is to rely on standard, fast models for general queries where deep reasoning is unnecessary. However, when tackling complex logic or when a standard model's initial attempt falls short, switching to a thinking model gives the system the opportunity to deduce the correct solution.

How to Activate 'Thinking Mode' in Different UIs

29:36
▶

When interacting with Grok, activating the reasoning capabilities requires a specific user interface action. In a new conversation, before submitting a prompt, users must explicitly select the think option. This instructs Grok to allocate additional time for processing and switches the underlying system to the reasoning model. Different providers will implement similar selectors, allowing users to choose between the newer thinking models or the previous generation of models that respond immediately without extended deliberation.

Introduction to Tool Use and Internet Search

30:00
▶

Up to this point, interactions with the language model have been restricted to text input and output. The model operates as an isolated, inert neural network—essentially a compressed artifact in a folder—whose sole function is to emit tokens based on its training data. The next critical capability to introduce is tool use, which allows the model to interact with the external world.

Internet search is a primary example of a highly effective tool. A common manual search workflow involves submitting queries to a search engine, opening multiple links, and sifting through pages often cluttered with advertisements to find a specific piece of information, such as a television show schedule. By equipping a language model with search capabilities, this entire process can be automated. The model is given the ability to execute searches, visit relevant web pages, extract their textual content, and load that data directly into its context window. It then synthesizes the gathered information to provide a concise, direct response, completely bypassing the fragmented experience of manual browsing.

How Internet Search Works for LLMs

31:32
▶

To enable an LLM to access current information, a mechanism is introduced where the model can emit a special token, acting as a command to search the internet. When this token is generated, the host application pauses the sampling process.

The application takes the search query formulated by the model, executes it, and scrapes the text from the resulting web pages. All of this scraped text—potentially thousands of tokens from multiple sources—is then concatenated and injected directly into the model's context window.

Once the web page content is loaded into this working memory, the model resumes sampling. It can now reference the newly retrieved text alongside the original user prompt to generate an accurate response. This tool is crucial for answering queries about recent events that occurred after the model's training cutoff date. Information that is not encoded within the model's parameters (its training data) must be fetched dynamically and placed into the context window for the model to process it.

Demonstrating Live Internet Search with Perplexity and ChatGPT

33:05

To overcome the limitation of static, pre-trained knowledge, Large Language Models can be augmented with external tools. A primary example is internet search, a feature popularized by applications like Perplexity.ai and subsequently integrated into ChatGPT. When queried for recent information, the model can either autonomously detect the need to search the web or be explicitly instructed to do so by the user.

During a search operation, the model generates queries, retrieves content from live web pages, and incorporates that external data back into its context window. It then synthesizes an answer based on this retrieved information. These systems typically provide citations linking back to the source web pages. This mechanism is critical for verification, allowing users to cross-check the facts and mitigate the risk of the model hallucinating details.

Comparing Search Capabilities Across Different LLMs

34:28
▶

When evaluating Large Language Models (LLMs) for tasks requiring current information, it is critical to determine whether the model has internet search capabilities integrated. Models behave differently when asked for information beyond their pre-training data.

For instance, some models like Grok may automatically execute a web search when they determine it is necessary to answer a prompt. Conversely, Claude currently lacks an integrated internet search tool. When asked for recent information, Claude will fall back to its knowledge cutoff date (e.g., April 2024) and state that it cannot provide a definitive answer.

Capabilities can also vary between model versions from the same provider. Within the Gemini ecosystem:

  • Gemini 2.0 Pro Experimental: This version lacks access to real-time information and the internet search tool, resulting in an inability to answer queries about recent events.
  • Gemini 2.0 Flash: This model is integrated with an internet search tool and can successfully retrieve current information, providing sources and related content alongside its response.

Recap of Internet Search and Tool Integration

36:21
▶

Models can be configured to perform web searches, visit web pages, and pull relevant information into their context window to answer questions. The level of integration for this capability varies across different models and applications. While some models automatically detect when a search is necessary, it can often be beneficial to explicitly instruct the model to perform a search. For instance, when a query is known to require recent or external information, manually selecting the search tool or explicitly requesting a search within the prompt can yield more reliable results.

Practical Examples of When to Use the Search Tool

37:01

The core heuristic for using an LLM equipped with a search tool is straightforward: if an answer can likely be found by performing a standard web search and synthesizing information from the top few links, a search-augmented workflow is appropriate. Services like Perplexity are explicitly designed for this exact use case.

There are several specific scenarios where relying on a search tool is strongly preferred over depending solely on an LLM's static internal weights:

  • Recent or frequently changing information: Models have a knowledge cutoff. Questions like Is the stock market open today? (which depends on the current date and holidays) or Does Vercel offer a PostgreSQL database? (as corporate offerings evolve) require up-to-date context. Queries about upcoming events, such as product launches or release dates, also fall into this category.
  • Niche or esoteric topics: If a topic is rarely mentioned on the internet, the model may not have encountered it frequently enough during pre-training to strongly encode the facts in its parameters. Questions like Where is White Lotus season 3 filmed? or specific personal details like What toothpaste does Bryan Johnson use? are prime examples. The search tool ensures the model retrieves the exact, specific documents needed rather than hallucinating based on weak internal associations.
  • Synthesizing current events or trending topics: When trying to understand a sudden trend on social media, individual posts often lack complete context. Searching for the broader topic and instructing the model to summarize the gist from recent articles provides a much clearer picture of unfolding events.

Introduction to Deep Research

39:01
▶

Deep research is a recently introduced capability that combines internet search with extended reasoning. It is currently available under the ChatGPT Pro tier, which requires a $200 per month subscription. Unlike standard queries, deep research allows the model to operate autonomously for tens of minutes, iteratively searching the web and processing information to compile comprehensive results.

Deep Research Demonstration: Researching a Supplement

39:57
▶

To demonstrate the capabilities of deep research, consider the task of investigating a specific health supplement, such as CAAKG (Calcium Alpha-Ketoglutarate), found in a commercial longevity mix. While a traditional approach involves manually querying search engines and reading through wikis or research papers, deep research automates the extraction and synthesis of this information.

An example prompt to initiate this research might look like:

CAAKG is one of the health actives in Bryan Johnson's blueprint at 2.5 grams per serving. Can you do research on CAAKG, tell me about why it might be found in the longevity mix, its possible efficacy in humans or animal models, its potential mechanism of action, and any potential concerns or toxicity?

Upon receiving a complex query, the model may first prompt the user with clarifying questions to narrow the scope of the research. For instance, it might ask whether to focus on human clinical studies, animal models, or both, and whether to restrict the search to specific sources. After these parameters are set, the model begins a prolonged research phase.

This process can take a significant amount of time (e.g., around 10 minutes) as the model performs an iterative cycle of internal reasoning and executing multiple internet searches to review various papers and articles. The interface typically provides a high-level preview of the agent's ongoing actions during this period.

Similar automated, multi-step research capabilities are available across other platforms in the industry. Alternatives offering comparable features include Perplexity, which has a deep research option available via its model dropdown, and Grok 3, which includes a Deep Search feature designed to replicate this intensive search behavior.

Analyzing the Deep Research Results

42:16
▶

The deep research capabilities of Grok, Perplexity, and ChatGPT involve accumulating a large context window by querying multiple sources, such as academic papers and medical websites, to generate comprehensive reports. These tools process the gathered information to provide detailed answers complete with citations, functioning similarly to a custom research paper generator.

When comparing the outputs of these models after extensive research periods, the structure of the reports typically covers:

  • Research findings across different models (e.g., worms, mice, and human trials).
  • Proposed mechanisms of action.
  • Safety considerations and potential concerns.
  • Extensive reference lists for further reading.

Based on initial evaluations, the deep research offering from ChatGPT consistently produces the most detailed and thorough reports. It analyzes a significant number of sources—often evaluating dozens of references for a single query—and provides the longest, most readable synthesis of the information. In contrast, the reports generated by Perplexity and Grok tend to be briefer and offer less depth compared to ChatGPT's current capabilities.

Caveats and Best Practices for Using Research Tools

44:20
▶

While research tools can pull in external information, there are no guarantees against hallucinations. The model may fabricate details or misunderstand the source material at any point. Because of this, provided citations are crucial. The model's output should be treated as a first draft and a collection of papers to investigate, rather than absolute truth.

A recommended workflow is to use the generated report to find relevant sources, then manually review the cited papers to verify the model's comprehension. This approach allows you to formulate follow-up questions and descend into the literature yourself, leveraging the model as a highly useful starting point rather than a final authority.

Practical Examples of Deep Research

45:03
▶

Deep research can be utilized for comparing different products or researching specific topics. For instance, when comparing browsers like Brave and Arc regarding privacy, a comprehensive report can highlight the differences, such as Brave being significantly more privacy-focused. This type of product comparison is well-suited for deep research. Another application is researching scientific topics, like life extension in animal models. A deep research query can yield a detailed overview of various techniques and lab results, providing a comprehensive understanding of the subject.

Evaluating LLM-Generated Tables: A Case of Failure

50:00

When prompting an LLM to generate factual summaries, such as a table of major U.S.-based LLM research labs, the output requires careful verification. In the generated table provided by the model, several inaccuracies and omissions are present, demonstrating a common failure mode in current systems. The most notable error is the complete omission of xAI, a major player in the space. Conversely, the inclusion of organizations like Hugging Face and EleutherAI is debatable depending on strict definitions of a "major LLM lab" based on compute resources and primary research focus. Furthermore, the specific figures for employee counts and total funding should be treated with skepticism until manually verified. While LLM-generated tables serve as an excellent first draft for organizing information, they cannot be fully trusted as authoritative, finalized sources.

Enhancing LLM Reliability with Concrete Documents

50:57

A key technique for improving LLM reliability is providing the model with concrete documents that it can reference directly within its context window. This approach shifts the model away from relying solely on the hazy, probabilistic knowledge stored within its parameters—what it "knows in its brain." By supplying specific documents, either through automated internet searches or direct file uploads, the LLM can anchor its responses in verified information, similar to a person consulting reference materials while formulating an answer. This functionality is particularly helpful for reducing hallucinations and ensuring factual accuracy.

Demonstration: Analyzing a Research Paper with File Upload

51:58
▶

Claude 3.7 features a thinking mode optimized for complex reasoning tasks, likely developed using reinforcement learning techniques similar to other recent reasoning models. A powerful application of these large context window models is the ability to upload and analyze external documents. By loading a complex document, such as the research paper "Genome modeling and design across all domains of life with Evo 2," directly into the context window, users can interactively query the text. This collaborative approach allows the LLM to act as a reading companion, making it highly effective for parsing and understanding dense material outside of one's immediate domain expertise.

Practical Application: Summarizing a PDF with an LLM

52:46
▶

To analyze a document, drag and drop the PDF directly into the LLM's chat interface. Under the hood, the PDF is typically converted into a raw text file before being loaded into the model's context window, which serves as its working memory. During this conversion, images and complex layouts are often discarded or poorly preserved, meaning the model relies almost entirely on the extracted text.

Once the document text is loaded into the token window, you can begin querying it. A common starting point is to ask the model to generate a summary. However, large documents can easily exceed the maximum token limit of a given model. If you encounter a length limit error, you may need to try a different model with a larger context window or use an application that implements chunking and retrieval strategies for long documents.

A New Way to Read: Using LLMs to Understand Complex Books

53:58

Engaging in a back-and-forth dialogue with an LLM while reading through the abstract and introduction of a paper makes it significantly easier to understand complex material. This collaborative reading approach is also highly effective for dense or historical books.

A practical example is reading Adam Smith's 1776 work, The Wealth of Nations. The workflow involves accessing the raw text—easily found on repositories like Project Gutenberg for public domain works—and providing the specific chapter currently being read to the model.

For instance, when reading a chapter on the division of labor, the raw text can be provided to the LLM to help break down the core arguments. The model can assist in unpacking concepts like how specialization is crucial for wealth creation by allowing individuals to become experts in specific tasks, and how this specialization is fundamentally limited by the scale of the available market.

Interactive Reading Workflow with LLMs

55:00
▶

To interactively read a dense or historical text, you can leverage an LLM by copying and pasting the chapter content directly into the prompt. While models like Claude have general knowledge of famous works like The Wealth of Nations, they do not perfectly memorize every chapter. Loading the specific text into the context window grounds the model's answers.

A typical workflow starts by providing the text and asking for a summary. For example, you can prompt the model with context like We are reading the wealth of nations. Please summarize this chapter to start. and then paste the chapter content. In Claude's interface, pasting a large amount of text automatically converts it into a file attachment, keeping the chat interface manageable.

After reviewing the initial summary to get a high-level understanding, you can begin reading the text itself. Whenever you encounter a confusing passage or an unfamiliar term, you return to the LLM to ask specific questions. This interactive process dramatically improves comprehension and retention. It makes it significantly more accessible to approach texts outside your area of expertise, such as scientific papers, very old documents, or archaic literature, by allowing you to work through confusing sections slowly with the LLM as a guide.

Currently, this workflow involves manually switching between the text source and the LLM interface. While an ideal tool would allow highlighting a passage and asking questions directly within a reading application, the manual copy-paste method remains an extremely effective way to study difficult material.

Introduction to the Python Interpreter Tool

56:00
▶

The Python interpreter tool grants the Language Model the ability to write and execute computer programs. Instead of providing a direct answer, the LLM emits special tokens that the ChatGPT application recognizes as a cue to execute the generated code. The application runs the program and returns the execution result back to the model.

This integration of the language model with a programming environment like Python is particularly useful for tasks that exceed the model's inherent capabilities, such as complex calculations. For a simple arithmetic problem like 30 * 9, the model can rely on next-token prediction to output the correct result directly, functioning much like human memory.

However, when presented with a highly complex multiplication problem, the model recognizes that it cannot compute the answer directly. OpenAI has trained ChatGPT to identify such scenarios and automatically fall back on tool use. In these cases, it writes a short Python script to perform the calculation, essentially using the Python interpreter as a calculator to find the precise answer.

How the Python Interpreter Works Behind the Scenes

57:11
▶

When ChatGPT opens the Python interpreter, it is using its ability to write and execute code. Instead of directly outputting a result, the language model writes a Python program. Special tokens, which are not displayed in the UI, instruct the ChatGPT application to run this program. The LLM then pauses its execution while the Python program runs and generates a result. This result is passed back to the language model as text, allowing it to resume and incorporate the outcome into its final response.

This mechanism of tool use is highly powerful. OpenAI has trained ChatGPT to recognize when it is appropriate to use tools, such as the Python interpreter. This training involves human labelers who curate datasets that provide examples of situations where tool use is necessary and how it should be executed. While simple calculations like multiplication are common, the capabilities within the programming environment extend far beyond basic arithmetic.

Comparing Tool Usage Across Different LLMs

58:01
▶

Different Large Language Models (LLMs) have varying access to external tools, such as Python interpreters or other programming environments. When an LLM lacks access to these tools—or is configured not to use them—it may attempt to solve complex deterministic problems, such as large mathematical calculations, internally. This often results in hallucinated answers that appear remarkably close to the truth but are fundamentally incorrect.

Observing different models handling calculation tasks highlights these discrepancies:

  • Grok 3: Operating without access to a programming language, the model attempted a calculation internally. It produced an incorrect, hallucinated answer (e.g., yielding a value ending in 060 instead of the correct 120), despite being numerically close.
  • Claude: Successfully executed the calculation by writing and running JavaScript code as a computational tool to obtain the correct result.
  • Gemini 2.0 Pro: Initially provided a correct answer without any indication of tool usage, suggesting a successful internal calculation. However, when tested with a significantly harder problem, the model returned an incorrect result (verified against a standard local calculator). This confirmed that it was relying on internal hallucination rather than deterministic computation.

It is necessary to track the specific tools available to the LLM being used. If an LLM does not have access to a reliable computational tool for a deterministic task, it will default to generating a best-effort text response, frequently leading to undetected hallucinations.

Advanced Data Analysis: A Powerful but Flawed Tool

59:34
▶

Advanced Data Analysis is a feature that acts as a junior data analyst, capable of writing and executing Python code to analyze and visualize data. However, interacting with this tool requires careful scrutiny of the code it generates.

A typical workflow might involve collecting and plotting data. For example, you can prompt the model to research an entity's valuation over time. It is recommended to explicitly instruct the model to use its search tool to gather real data rather than hallucinating numbers. Once a table of valuations is generated, you can ask it to plot the data, such as using a log scale for the Y-axis. The model will write a script to create and display the figure.

While powerful, the generated code often contains hidden flaws that necessitate manual review:

  • Implicit assumptions: If a data point is missing (e.g., an "NA" value for a specific year), the code might silently impute a value, such as setting it to 0.1, without notifying the user.
  • Textual hallucinations contradicting code: When asked to fit a trend line (using tools like scipy.optimize.curve_fit) and extrapolate future values, the model might produce a text response that completely contradicts the output of its own code. In one instance, the text claimed an extrapolated 2030 valuation of 1.7 trillion, while the variable printed on the generated plot clearly showed 20271.7 B (approximately 20 trillion). The model hallucinated the text response despite calculating a different value in the code.

Because the model can make silent modifications or hallucinate summaries of its own analyses, it is critical that users possess the ability to read and verify the underlying code it produces.

Introducing Claude Artifacts: Creating a Flashcard App

61:00

Following the use of Advanced Data Analysis to execute code and perform calculations within the chat interface, another powerful paradigm is introduced by Claude's Artifacts feature.

# Calculate the product of 423534 and 3124
result = 423534 * 3124
result

Claude Artifacts allows the LLM to write and render interactive web applications directly in the browser. A practical example involves generating a custom flashcard application. By providing source text—such as an excerpt from a Wikipedia page—Claude can first generate a list of question-and-answer pairs.

You can then prompt Claude to use the artifacts feature to create an interactive app to test these flashcards. Claude writes the required frontend code, often utilizing React components, and hardcodes the generated Q&A data into the application state. The interface instantly renders these components, resulting in a functional, custom-built web app where you can click to reveal answers, track correctness, and shuffle cards.

This approach contrasts with traditional software models where you download a generic application (like Anki) and import custom data. Here, the LLM generates a bespoke application entirely on demand. These artifact applications are typically local and run entirely within the browser without a backend database, but they can achieve significant complexity and utility for specialized, single-use tasks.

Using Claude Artifacts to Generate Conceptual Diagrams

62:44

A highly practical use case for Claude's Artifacts feature is generating conceptual diagrams from text. When provided with a document, such as a chapter from a book, Claude can analyze the content and create a visual representation of its structure.

While Claude can generate simple scripts to solve basic queries—such as executing a calculation directly:

// Calculate the product of 423534 and 3124
const product = 423534 * 3124;
console.log(product);

—its ability to synthesize complex information is more effectively demonstrated through diagramming. When prompted to create a conceptual diagram of a text like a chapter from Adam Smith's The Wealth of Nations, Claude generates graph definition code using the Mermaid library. The resulting visualization maps out the core arguments, illustrating relationships such as how the division of labor connects to the extent of the market, supported by comparisons of land versus water transport and their historical impact on early civilizations.

Laying out information as a spatial tree structure is an effective technique for visual thinkers to understand and retain the layout of a complex argument. This diagramming approach can be applied broadly to books, individual chapters, and even source code to quickly grasp structural relationships.

The Best Tools for Professional Coding with LLMs

64:02
▶

While LLMs are highly capable of writing code, interacting with them through web interfaces like ChatGPT or Claude for professional development is often inefficient. Although these platforms are adding features to run code or render components directly in the browser (like Claude Artifacts), they lack the necessary context of an entire codebase required for serious software engineering.

Instead of relying on web chat interfaces for code snippets, the industry standard for professional coding with LLMs involves using specialized, AI-integrated IDEs. Tools like Cursor, Windsurf, or extensions for VS Code are designed to provide the LLM with deeper context about the project, enabling a much faster and more effective workflow than copying and pasting snippets from a browser window.

Introduction to Cursor AI Code Editor

75:00

Currently, Cursor is a preferred AI code editor. It is a standalone application downloaded to your computer, such as a MacBook, rather than a web-based interface. Cursor operates directly on your local file system, referencing and editing the files alongside you. For example, you can quickly build a React application using Cursor, which, under the hood, utilizes the Claude 3.5 Sonnet model.

Cursor's AI Backend and Tic-Tac-Toe Demo

75:30

Cursor operates by calling backend AI APIs, such as Anthropic's Claude, to perform code generation and editing tasks. The key advantage is that the editor automatically manages the context of the files in the working directory. This eliminates the need to manually copy and paste code chunks between the editor and a separate web interface.

As an example, the AI assistant generated a functional React Tic-Tac-Toe application. The generated code includes standard logic for state management and win condition checking.

import React, { useState } from 'react';
import './App.css';

function App() {
  // Create a state to track the board (null = empty, 'X' or 'O')
  const [board, setBoard] = useState<(string | null)[]>(Array(9).fill(null));
  // Track whose turn it is
  const [isXNext, setIsXNext] = useState<boolean>(true);

  // Calculate the winner
  const calculateWinner = (squares: (string | null)[]) => {
    const lines = [
      [0, 1, 2], // top row
      [3, 4, 5], // middle row
      [6, 7, 8], // bottom row
      [0, 3, 6], // left column
      [1, 4, 7], // middle column
      [2, 5, 8], // right column
      [0, 4, 8], // diagonal
      [2, 4, 6], // diagonal
    ];

    for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) {
      const [a, b, c] = lines[i];
      if (squares[a] && squares[a] === squares[b] && squares[a] === squares[c]) {
        return squares[a];
      }
    }
    return null;
  };

  // Get the current game status
  const winner = calculateWinner(board);
  let status;
  if (winner) {
    status = `Winner: ${winner}`;

How Cursor Built the App (Composer Feature)

76:55

Cursor's Composer feature allows for rapid generation of full applications by providing high-level natural language instructions. By simply requesting to set up a new React repository, delete boilerplate, and create a simple Tic Tac Toe app, Cursor generated all the necessary CSS, JavaScript, and React components without requiring manual coding.

import React, { useState } from 'react';
import './App.css';

function App() {
  // Create a state to track the board (null = empty, 'X' or 'O')
  const [board, setBoard] = useState<(string | null)[]>(Array(9).fill(null));
  // Track whose turn it is
  const [isXNext, setIsXNext] = useState<boolean>(true);

  // Calculate the winner
  const calculateWinner = (squares: (string | null)[]) => {
    const lines = [
      [0, 1, 2], // top row
      [3, 4, 5], // middle row
      [6, 7, 8], // bottom row
      [0, 3, 6], // left column
      [1, 4, 7], // middle column
      [2, 5, 8], // right column
      [0, 4, 8], // diagonal
      [2, 4, 6], // diagonal
    ];
    for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) {
      const [a, b, c] = lines[i];
      if (squares[a] && squares[a] === squares[b] && squares[a] === squares[c]) {
        return squares[a];
      }
    }
    return null;
  };

  // Get the current game status
  const winner = calculateWinner(board);
  let status;
  if (winner) {
    status = `Winner: ${winner}`;
  } else if (board.every(cell => cell !== null)) {

The editor hosts the application locally and interacts directly with the API under the hood, utilizing the full context of the repository's files. These built-in tools have progressively evolved to become more elaborate:

  • Command K: Used to target and modify specific lines of code.
  • Command L: Invokes an LLM to explain a selected chunk of code, leveraging the editor's access to all local files for full context.
  • Command I: Accesses the Composer tool, which can generate and orchestrate multiple files and changes across an entire project.

The Concept of "Vibe Coding"

78:08

With the Composer agent integration, the assistant acts autonomously on the codebase, executing commands and editing across multiple files as needed. This workflow introduces the concept of vibe coding: delegating control to the Composer, providing high-level instructions, and trusting the agent to implement them correctly.

While the agent handles the low-level programming tasks, the standard development fallback remains available. If the agent's output is incorrect or needs refinement, you retain full access to inspect and modify all source files arbitrarily, such as the React components or CSS. The current state of the application being generated by the agent is a standard Tic-Tac-Toe implementation in React.

import React, { useState } from 'react';
import './App.css';

function App() {
  // Create a state to track the board (null = empty, 'X' or 'O')
  const [board, setBoard] = useState<(string | null)[]>(Array(9).fill(null));
  // Track whose turn it is
  const [isXNext, setIsXNext] = useState<boolean>(true);

  // Calculate the winner
  const calculateWinner = (squares: (string | null)[]) => {
    const lines = [
      [0, 1, 2], // top row
      [3, 4, 5], // middle row
      [6, 7, 8], // bottom row
      [0, 3, 6], // left column
      [1, 4, 7], // middle column
      [2, 5, 8], // right column
      [0, 4, 8], // diagonal
      [2, 4, 6], // diagonal
    ];

    for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) {
      const [a, b, c] = lines[i];
      if (squares[a] && squares[a] === squares[b] && squares[a] === squares[c]) {
        return squares[a];
      }
    }
    return null;
  };

  // Get the current game status
  const winner = calculateWinner(board);
  let status;
  if (winner) {
    status = 'Winner: ' + winner;
  } else if (!board.includes(null)) {

Live Coding Demo: Adding Confetti to the Game

78:44

When prompting the AI agent to add a visual feature like a victory confetti animation, it can independently identify and recommend suitable external libraries. In this instance, the agent suggests using the react-confetti package and provides the necessary terminal command for installation.

npm install react-confetti

After you approve and run the installation, the agent automatically proceeds to generate the required code and updates the main application file (App.tsx) to implement the effect.

Cursor's Code Generation and Styling

79:27

The model generates multiple chunks of code across the file to implement the requested features. First, it imports the react-confetti library and sets up the necessary state variables, including tracking window dimensions to render the confetti correctly and a boolean to toggle the animation.

import React, { useState, useEffect } from 'react';
import Confetti from 'react-confetti';

// ...
  // State for window dimensions (for confetti)
  const [windowDimensions, setWindowDimensions] = useState<{ width: number; height: number }>({
    width: window.innerWidth,
    height: window.innerHeight,
  });
  // State to control confetti animation
  const [showConfetti, setShowConfetti] = useState(false);

  // Update window dimensions when window is resized
  useEffect(() => {
    const handleResize = () => {
      setWindowDimensions({
        width: window.innerWidth,
        height: window.innerHeight,
      });
    };

    window.addEventListener('resize', handleResize);
    return () => {
      window.removeEventListener('resize', handleResize);
    };
  }, []);

Simultaneously, the agent begins modifying the logic to identify winning cells for styling. It updates the return structure of the win calculation to provide not just the winning player, but also the specific indices of the winning line.

    const [a, b, c] = lines[i];
    if (squares[a] && squares[a] === squares[b] && squares[a] === squares[c]) {
      return {
        winner: squares[a],
        winningLine: [a, b, c]
      };
    }
  }
  return null;

The agent then queues up additional CSS generation to visually highlight these winning cells based on the updated logic.

Adding Sound Effects and Live Preview

80:03
▶

The agent proposes adding a sound effect when a player wins. This seems ambitious because it is unclear where the agent will source the sound file from. However, because the application deploys every time a file is saved, the current state can be tested by refreshing the live preview.

Upon refreshing, a new fade-in effect is visible. After playing a game to a win, the confetti effect successfully triggers. The agent has paused execution and is waiting for confirmation on a command, indicating it has completed a step and is ready for the next instruction.

Finalizing the Sound Effect Feature

80:54

The AI agent proceeds to create the necessary directory for the sound file using a shell command:

$ mkdir -p public/sounds

It then generates the React code to play the audio file when a player wins. This code is placed inside a useEffect hook that triggers when the winner state changes, alongside the existing confetti logic:

  // Show confetti when there's a winner
  useEffect(() => {
    if (winner) {
      setShowConfetti(true);

      // Play victory sound
      const audio = new Audio('/sounds/victory.mp3');
      audio.volume = 0.5; // Set volume to 50%
      audio.play().catch(e => console.log('Error playing sound:', e));

      // Stop confetti after 5 seconds
      const timer = setTimeout(() => {
        setShowConfetti(false);
      }, 5000);
      return () => clearTimeout(timer);
    }
  }, [winner]);

Recognizing that the victory.mp3 file does not yet exist locally, the agent automatically formulates a curl command to download a sample sound effect from a public asset repository:

$ curl -o public/sounds/victory.mp3 https://assets.mixkit.co/active_storage/sfx/2000/2000-preview.mp3

With the sound file downloaded and the fallback error handling included, the feature implementation is complete and ready to be committed to version control.

The Final Result and "Vibe Coding" in Action

81:44

With the composer completing its task, the application is ready for a test run. The final result successfully integrates the confetti animation and plays a victory sound upon winning, as specified in the prompt. The source of the audio file used by the model is unclear, likely drawn from its training data where similar URLs might appear frequently in repositories.

curl -o public/sounds/victory.mp3 https://assets.mixkit.co/active_storage/sfx/2000/2000-preview.mp3

This demonstrates the capability to rapidly iterate on an application using AI assistance. Should the "vibe coding" approach fail to resolve a complex issue, the developer can always revert to traditional debugging and programming methods.

Interacting with Models via Text vs. Other Modalities

82:31

Up to this point, interactions with the model have been exclusively text-based. Expanding the interaction model to include other modalities allows for communication in more native human formats. This involves handling inputs and outputs beyond text, specifically encompassing speech, audio, images, and video.

The immediate focus shifts to speech and audio modalities, exploring how to establish voice-based interactions. As outlined in the architecture diagram, tooling for audio input includes applications like Superwhisper, WisprFlow, and MacWhisper, while audio output is typically handled natively within the respective AI application.

Using Speech-to-Text on Mobile (ChatGPT App)

83:08

Voice input is highly utilized, especially on mobile devices where typing can be cumbersome. The ChatGPT mobile application features two distinct voice modes, indicated by separate icons in the interface.

The microphone icon facilitates speech-to-text transcription. Activating this mode prompts the application to listen to spoken audio and transcribe it directly into text within the message input field. This allows users to bypass the keyboard entirely for query entry. The workflow is straightforward: initiate a new conversation, tap the microphone icon, speak the prompt, and the application converts the audio to text, which can then be submitted to the model.

Speech-to-Text on Desktop (SuperWhisper)

84:27

On the desktop version of ChatGPT, audio input is slightly different. While there is a Voice Mode, there is no direct microphone icon for simple speech-to-text transcription within the text input field. To bypass this limitation, you can use system-wide transcription applications that capture your audio and convert it to text anywhere on your computer.

Several apps provide this functionality on macOS, including:

  • SuperWhisper
  • WisprFlow
  • MacWhisper

Using an app like SuperWhisper involves binding a global hotkey (such as F5). When you press the hotkey, the app begins recording. After you finish speaking, pressing the hotkey again stops the recording, and the app automatically transcribes the audio and pastes the resulting text directly into your active window, such as the ChatGPT input box.

Text-to-Speech and "Fake Audio"

85:40
▶

Voice input is highly efficient for simple queries, allowing for rapid interaction without manual typing. However, when queries involve specific product names, library names, or technical jargon that may not transcribe accurately, manual typing remains the preferred method to ensure correctness.

On the output side, many LLM applications offer Text-to-Speech (TTS) functionality, performing the inverse operation of converting generated text back into audio. For example, in the ChatGPT interface, a "Read aloud" icon is available beneath the generated response. Clicking this icon passes the text to a TTS model, which then plays the audio back to the user.

While this functionality is integrated into some applications, its availability varies across different platforms. For system-wide voice input, native OS features or third-party tools can be used to dictate text into any application. Conversely, for system-wide text-to-speech capabilities—having the OS read text out loud from any context—a dedicated system-wide TTS application may need to be configured.

Introduction to "True Audio"

86:45

Up to this point, interactions utilizing voice inputs have been what can be described as "fake audio." In these systems, the interaction with the core language model remains strictly text-based. Separate speech-to-text or text-to-speech models are employed to pre-process audio into text before it is fed to the model, and to convert the model's text output back into audio. The language model itself is only ever processing text tokens.

The alternative is "true audio," where audio is handled natively inside the language model. Just as text is chunked into discrete tokens, other modalities like audio can be tokenized. A common approach involves converting the audio into a spectrogram to represent its frequency content over time. This continuous representation is then divided into small windows and quantized into discrete tokens, drawing from a vocabulary of potentially hundreds of thousands of possible audio chunks. By training the model directly on these audio tokens, it develops a native understanding of the audio segments, unlocking capabilities that are impossible when relying on an intermediate text translation.

ChatGPT's Advanced Voice Mode ("True Audio") Demo

87:47

OpenAI's Advanced Voice Mode implements "true audio" capabilities, meaning that voice processing is handled natively within the language model. Instead of relying on intermediate speech-to-text and text-to-speech pipelines, the model is trained to directly understand and predict audio chunks. This allows for seamless interactions without any text intermediation in the processing loop.

In practice, this native audio understanding allows the model to respond dynamically to voice inputs, including adopting specific vocal personas or styles on the fly, such as explaining factual concepts while mimicking a requested character's voice.

Voice Impersonations and Model Capabilities

88:52
▶

The voice model demonstrates flexibility in adopting different personas and speaking styles. It can transition between distinct character voices, such as mimicking Yoda or a pirate, while maintaining the semantic content of its explanation—in this case, the reason the sky appears blue. Additionally, the model can adopt a narrative, storytelling tone to explain concepts in an engaging manner.

Beyond character impersonations, the model's speech characteristics can be manipulated through prompting. It is capable of accelerating its speaking rate, as demonstrated by the prompt to count from 1 to 20 as fast as possible. This highlights the dynamic nature of the generated audio, incorporating stylistic and pacing adjustments based on user instructions. The model's capabilities also extend to generating non-speech audio, such as animal sounds.

Testing the Limits: Animal Sounds and Model Refusals

90:06
▶

When testing the limits of the model's audio capabilities, it can be prompted to imitate animal sounds. While it might successfully produce a sound like a cow's "moo," it may refuse other requests, such as imitating a fox, stating it can only describe sounds. Even when challenged on this inconsistency, the model may persist in its refusal. Following an audio conversation, ChatGPT transcribes the interaction into text, making it available for review.

Understanding the "True Audio" Process

91:07
▶

When using the advanced voice mode, the interaction occurs entirely via audio tokens. There is no intermediate step where audio is converted to text for processing; the text displayed in the chat interface is merely a post-hoc transcription of the audio conversation. While technically impressive, the current iteration of the advanced voice model can be quite restrictive and frequently refuses prompts. Additionally, feature availability and access tiers evolve rapidly, so functionalities like advanced voice mode may become accessible across different user tiers over time.

Grok's Advanced Voice Mode and Unhinged Personas

92:01
▶

Grok also offers an advanced voice mode functionality, though it is currently limited to the mobile application and is unavailable on the desktop web interface. This feature is accessed via a voice icon located in the top right corner of the app.

A significant characteristic of Grok is its variety of interaction modes, including some designed to be unrestricted or "unhinged." In contrast to OpenAI's models, which often employ strict guardrails and refuse certain prompts, Grok is generally more permissive. This less restrictive approach makes the Grok app a viable alternative for users prioritizing entertainment.

Grok Voice Demo: Default and Romantic Modes

92:57
▶

The Grok voice interface includes selectable persona modes that fundamentally alter the style, tone, and character of the generated audio. When interacting with the default mode, the assistant provides standard, friendly conversational replies. Switching to the romantic mode shifts the persona to a character named "Era," generating outputs characterized by hesitant speech patterns, simulated nervousness complete with filler words, and an affectionate tone. The interface also includes an unhinged mode for further stylistic variation.

Grok Voice Demo: Unhinged and Conspiracy Modes

93:55
▶

The voice interface for Grok includes various persona modes that drastically alter its conversational style. The "Unhinged" mode employs aggressive, profane language and a confrontational tone, responding to prompts with outright hostility. Alternatively, the "Conspiracy" mode adopts an enthusiastic, informal persona eager to discuss and validate fringe theories, such as the claim that Justin Trudeau is the son of Fidel Castro.

Grok Voice Demo: Sexy Mode and Final Thoughts

95:02
▶

The demonstration continues by exploring different conversational tones available in the Grok voice interface. After engaging in a playful exchange about various conspiracy theories, including political dynasties and shadow governments, the model is switched to a "sexy mode." In this mode, the voice assistant adopts an overly suggestive and intimate persona, illustrating the range of styles the model can emulate. Following this demonstration of voice-based interaction, the focus shifts to another audio-based paradigm: Google's NotebookLM.

Introduction to NotebookLM for Audio Interaction

96:09
▶

NotebookLM allows users to upload arbitrary data sources, such as raw text, web pages, and PDF files, which are then loaded into the model's context window. Once the sources are uploaded, the interface provides two primary methods for interacting with the data:

  1. Chat: Users can query the uploaded information directly to ask questions and receive answers.
  2. Deep Dive Podcast: A generation feature that creates a custom audio podcast discussing the provided sources. This synthesizes the information into a conversational format, providing an alternative way to passively consume the uploaded material.

Example of a Generated Podcast

97:02

NotebookLM includes an Audio Overview feature that generates a multi-speaker, podcast-style discussion based on the provided source documents. The generation process can be guided by providing special instructions to customize the focus of the conversation. The interface also includes an Interactive mode, which allows the user to interrupt the ongoing audio playback to ask questions dynamically.

This functionality is well-suited for creating custom audio content on arbitrary or niche topics, such as specific academic papers, that are unlikely to be covered by existing human-created podcasts. It provides a convenient way to consume complex information passively, for example, while walking or driving.

Creating a Full Podcast Season with AI

98:09
▶

A full podcast season titled Histories of Mysteries was generated by providing a selection of topics of interest. The tool created deep dive audio content for each topic, which was then uploaded to Spotify.

Representing Images as Tokens for Language Models

100:00
▶

Following the generation of audio content, the same tokenization principles can be extended to images. Images can be re-represented as token streams, allowing language models to process them using the exact same modeling techniques applied to text and audio. A basic approach involves dividing an image into a rectangular grid, effectively chopping it into a sequence of small patches.

To convert these patches into discrete tokens, a quantization process is applied. A vocabulary of possible patches is established—for instance, a vocabulary containing 100,000 distinct patch patterns. Each patch from the source image is then represented by the closest matching patch in this vocabulary. This process yields a discrete stream of tokens that can be inserted into a model's context window for training.

The core transformer neural network remains completely agnostic to the origin of these tokens. It does not natively distinguish whether the tokens represent text, audio snippets, or image patches; its sole function is to model the statistical patterns within the token sequences. The modality-specific logic is isolated to the input encoder, which translates raw data into the token stream, and the output decoder, which reconstructs the final image, audio, or text from the predicted tokens. Across different modalities, only the token stream format and the token vocabulary change.

Analyzing a Nutrition Label with Image Input

100:15

When providing image inputs to a large language model, a robust workflow involves breaking the interaction into two steps. First, instruct the model to transcribe the relevant information from the image into structured text, such as a table. This step allows you to verify that the model correctly extracted the values, effectively acting as an Optical Character Recognition (OCR) check to ensure accuracy.

Second, once the transcription is verified, ask analytical questions based on that text. For instance, when examining a complex nutrition label, you can prompt the model to group the transcribed ingredients or rank them by safety or prevalence, distinguishing between standard multivitamin components and more esoteric compounds. This two-step process ensures the analysis is grounded in verified data and serves as a solid foundation for further research.

Interpreting Blood Test Results with an LLM

100:52

Large PDF reports, such as extensive blood test results, can be tedious to parse manually. By taking targeted screenshots of specific sections, such as a lipid panel, and providing them to the model, you can leverage its image input modalities for interpretation.

A highly efficient workflow on macOS is to use Control + Shift + Command + 4 to capture a specific screen region directly to the clipboard. You can then use Command + V to paste the image directly into the chat interface. Uploading a long document piece by piece and verifying the model's transcription of the data ensures higher accuracy than providing the entire file at once.

While querying medical information requires caution, standard diagnostic panels are heavily represented in the model's training data. The model generally understands the relevant numbers, standard ranges, and biomarkers well enough to provide a robust initial analysis. This serves as a useful baseline interpretation to inform a more detailed conversation with a medical professional.

Miscellaneous Image Input Examples

101:42
▶

Image inputs can be utilized across a variety of practical and analytical tasks:

  • Math and Code Extraction: Complex equations or code snippets from papers can be photographed and transcribed into text or LaTeX format. This allows the extracted expressions to be evaluated or manipulated further by the model.
  • Product Ingredient Analysis: By uploading an image of a product's ingredient list, the model can transcribe the contents, explain the function of each chemical, and evaluate which ingredients are functionally essential versus those that are potentially unnecessary additives.
  • Meme Explanation: Visual humor often relies on implicit knowledge. The model can analyze an image, identify the subjects, and explain the underlying joke—for instance, recognizing that a group of crows is called a "murder" to decode a visual pun about an "attempted murder."

Image Generation with DALL-E 3

102:23
▶

These models can also generate images. OpenAI's offering for this is DALL-E 3, which can produce highly stylistic images based on arbitrary prompts. A common practical application is generating assets like icons or thumbnails for YouTube videos. There are also competing tools available, such as ideogram.ai, which can be used interchangeably depending on the desired visual results.

Under the hood, image generation in the current iteration of ChatGPT is not handled entirely by the main large language model. When a user requests an image, the model first generates a detailed text caption based on the user's prompt. This newly generated caption is then sent out to a separate, dedicated text-to-image model to produce the final visual output.

Live Demonstration of Video Input

103:30
▶

The advanced voice mode, available on the mobile application, includes functionality for live video input. By activating the video icon during a voice session, the user can share their camera feed, allowing the model to observe the physical environment and respond to questions about it.

In a demonstration of this capability, the model successfully identified and discussed several objects in real-time:

  • Acoustic foam panels, suggesting alternative placement strategies for better sound absorption.
  • Specific books based on their covers, including Genghis Khan and the Making of the Modern World and Surely You're Joking, Mr. Feynman!, providing brief summaries of each.
  • An Aranet4 portable CO2 monitor. The model identified the device, read the current measurement of 713 ppm, and confirmed that this is an acceptable indoor level.
  • A map of Middle Earth from The Lord of the Rings.

This interface enables highly natural, conversational interactions about the physical world. While the user experience feels akin to continuous video streaming, the underlying implementation likely samples the video feed at a specific framerate—extracting individual image frames periodically (e.g., one frame per second) for the model to process. This point-and-ask functionality offers an exceptionally intuitive interaction paradigm, particularly useful for users who prefer natural dialogue over targeted text queries.

Overview of AI Video Generation Models

104:43
▶

The landscape of AI video generation models is rapidly evolving, providing highly capable tools for creative applications. Various models are available, each offering slightly different styles and qualities. Notable examples include VO2, which operates near the current state-of-the-art, and OpenAI's Sora. Comparing the outputs of these dedicated models can help determine the best tool for a specific use case.

The ChatGPT Memory Feature Explained

105:20
▶

By default, every new conversation with ChatGPT begins with an empty token window; the context from previous sessions is completely wiped clean. To allow the model to retain information across sessions, ChatGPT implements a Memory feature.

This feature can be invoked explicitly by instructing the model to remember a specific detail or preference during a conversation (e.g., asking it to remember this). In some cases, ChatGPT will also trigger a memory update automatically based on natural interaction. When a memory is stored, the model generates a concise summary of the newly learned information and records it in its memory bank.

The memory bank functions as a localized database of knowledge about the user. The contents of this database are persistently prepended to the context window of all subsequent conversations. This mechanism ensures the model has ongoing access to learned user context, enabling it to provide more personalized and relevant responses over time without requiring the user to restate their preferences in every new chat.
